metadata.title: What is inside the Brutalist agentic system exactly?
metadata.purpose: Teardown of the two-axis taxonomy organizing the Brutalist agentic system. Teaches SPECIFIES vs INFLUENCES, INPUTS vs ARTIFACTS, the input stack, conditioning text stacking, artifact classes, and the judgment rule. Thesis: the determinism boundary IS the human-judgment boundary.
metadata.key_case: One instruction, forty files moved: fps=24 ran deterministically; register:Teardown ran through an underdetermined pass gated for taste — that split is the whole taxonomy.
deferred: Multi-agent scheduling and inter-agent communication protocols
Token budget allocation across orchestrator and doers
How the gate structure changes with asynchronous human review
Caching and versioning in the specifying half of the stack
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'BrutalistTerminalOpen' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B_OUTRO: palette=claude but the outro is 'NikBearBrownOutro' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. One instruction. Forty files moved. Audio generated, diagrams rendered, video compiled. What actually ran? Not one thing — a cascade. And every step in that cascade falls into one of two categories. That split is what this is about.

props.command: $ art explainer 'What is inside the Brutalist agentic system exactly?'

beats.narration_text: Here's the cascade. The orchestrator reads the beat sheet — the contract. Calls a doer. The doer calls ElevenLabs with an exact voice ID. Manim renders frames at exactly 24fps. Compile stitches them. One instruction — very different mechanisms at each step.

production_viz.label: beat_sheet → orchestrator → doer → [ElevenLabs | Manim] → compile → mp4
production_viz.mechanic: Left-to-right flow. Boxes in INK: 'beat_sheet.json' (label CONTRACT), 'orchestrator', 'doer', then a branch — top arm to 'ElevenLabs' (label 'voice_id — exact'), bottom arm to 'Manim' (label 'fps=24 — exact'), both merge to 'compile', then 'mp4' (CRIMSON border, label 'taste-judged'). Boxes reveal left to right with their connecting arrows. Parameter values in PT Mono below each step.

beats.narration_text: Compare two inputs in that cascade. fps=24 is a command — you get 24 frames per second, every run, guaranteed. register:Teardown is a bias — I can't tell you what sentence comes out, only that it won't be hype. Two inputs. Completely different structures.

production_viz.label: fps=24 (specifying) vs register:Teardown (influencing) — side by side
production_viz.mechanic: Split frame. Left card (INK border, label 'SPECIFIES'): 'fps = 24' in PT Mono; equals sign below it; 'output: 24 frames/sec · guaranteed · reproducible'. Right card (SLATE border, label 'INFLUENCES'): 'register: Teardown' in PT Mono; tilde '~' below; three greyed output lines with a crimson arrow pointing to one: 'output: ??? · emergent · unjudgeable by schema'. Left card reveals first, right card second.

beats.narration_text: That's the first axis: SPECIFIES versus INFLUENCES. A specifying input names its exact output — a hex code, an fps, a voice ID. Reproducible, versionable. An influencing input biases a distribution — a persona, a prompt. The output is emergent.

props.caption: axis 1 — the determinism split
props.code:
# SPECIFIES — names the exact output
fps      = 24                 # same in → same out
voice_id = 'VOICE_NBB'        # same in → same out
hex_code = '#C8102E'          # same in → same out

# INFLUENCES — biases a distribution
register = 'Teardown'         # same in → emergent out
persona  = 'script-writer'    # same in → emergent out
prompt   = '...'              # same in → emergent out

beats.narration_text: Even at temperature zero, a persona is unlike a hex code. The hex code overdetermines its output — one right answer. The persona underdetermines it — many acceptable answers, the model picks one. Not a temperature problem. A structure problem.

production_viz.label: overdetermined (hex code) vs underdetermined (persona)
production_viz.mechanic: Two columns side by side. Left column — 'OVERDETERMINED': one input box (CRIMSON, label '#C8102E') at top; single thick arrow pointing to one output box: 'one right answer'. Right column — 'UNDERDETERMINED': one input box (SLATE, label 'register: Teardown') at top; five arrows fanning to five output boxes (greyed); one box highlighted with crimson arrow and label 'model selects'. Below each column: 'hex code' (left) and 'persona' (right) in small serif. Footer: 'Not a temperature problem. A structure problem.' centered in italic EB Garamond.

beats.narration_text: Second axis: INPUTS versus ARTIFACTS. Inputs are authored before the run — personas, parameters, scripts. Artifacts are what the run leaves behind — the beat sheet, the mp4, the logs. These axes are independent. A beat sheet is both: input to compile, artifact of planning.

props.caption: axis 2 — before-the-run vs produced-by-the-run
props.code:
# INPUTS — authored before the run
before_run = [persona, parameters, scripts]

# ARTIFACTS — what the run leaves behind
produced   = [beat_sheet_json, mp4, logs]

# axes are INDEPENDENT — the same object can be both:
# beat_sheet.json = input(to='compile') + artifact(of='planning')
assert beat_sheet in before_run and beat_sheet in produced

beats.narration_text: Two axes. Four quadrants. A voice ID is a specifying input. A persona is an influencing input. The mp4 is exact in its bits — but no schema validates that it's good. The taxonomy isn't about file type. It's about who reads it and what judgment it invites.

production_viz.label: 2×2 matrix: SPECIFIES/INFLUENCES × INPUT/ARTIFACT
production_viz.mechanic: A 2×2 matrix. Y-axis (vertical): 'SPECIFIES' (top, INK) vs 'INFLUENCES' (bottom, SLATE). X-axis (horizontal): 'INPUT' (left) vs 'ARTIFACT' (right). Quadrant contents draw in one at a time: TL — 'voice_id · fps=24 · hex_code'; BL — 'persona · register · prompt'; TR — 'beat_sheet.json · compile output'; BR — 'mp4' with a CRIMSON corner triangle marker and label 'taste-judged — no schema possible'. Axis lines in INK. Labels in Montserrat tracked caps. The BR corner mark is the visual anchor — arrives last.

beats.narration_text: The input stack — six layers, top to bottom, from most influencing to most specifying. Human. LLM. Conditioning text. Agent. Parameters. Scripts. Not a hierarchy of importance. A hierarchy of underdetermination.

production_viz.label: 6-layer input stack, most influencing (top) → most specifying (bottom)
production_viz.mechanic: Six horizontal bars stacking top to bottom, each with a label, revealing staggered. Bar 1 HUMAN: darkest INK fill, widest, label 'HUMAN — goal · taste · irreplaceable'. Bar 2 LLM: slightly lighter INK, label 'LLM — distribution over text'. Bar 3 CONDITIONING TEXT: SLATE, label 'CONDITIONING TEXT — persona × register × brand'. Bar 4 AGENT: SLATE, label 'AGENT — orchestrator + doer'. Bar 5 PARAMETERS: lighter SLATE, label 'PARAMETERS — fps · voice_id · stability'. Bar 6 SCRIPTS: lightest, label 'SCRIPTS — generate_audio.py · compile.py'. Left margin: 'MOST INFLUENCING' in CRIMSON at top, 'MOST SPECIFYING' in INK at bottom. Bars shrink slightly as the stack descends to show decreasing underdetermination.

beats.narration_text: The human is at the top because only the human carries goal and taste. The LLM produces a distribution over outputs — no goal of its own. The human wants something specific. That asymmetry is why the human is irreplaceable. Not by choice. By structure.

props.command: $ why is the human irreplaceable at the top of the stack?
output: human:     carries goal + taste  ← irreplaceable BY STRUCTURE
llm:       distribution over text ← no goal, no stakes
gap:       human wants something specific — LLM does not
verdict:   no agent in the stack can substitute for this

beats.narration_text: The LLM is raw capability — a distribution over text. Conditioning text steers it. Three sub-types stack: method persona says WHAT to produce — script-writer, explainer, bio. Voice register says HOW it sounds — Teardown, sardonic, wonder. Brand says WHOSE voice — NikBearBrown, Hai, Medhavy.

production_viz.label: conditioning text: method persona × register × brand — three sub-types that stack
production_viz.mechanic: Three boxes appear left to right with plus signs between them, then compress into one 'CONDITIONING TEXT' bar. Box 1 (SLATE): 'METHOD PERSONA' header, examples 'script-writer · explainer · bio', sub-label '(WHAT to produce)'. Box 2 (SLATE): 'VOICE REGISTER' header, examples 'Teardown · sardonic · wonder', sub-label '(HOW it sounds)'. Box 3 (SLATE): 'BRAND' header, examples 'NikBearBrown · Hai · Medhavy', sub-label '(WHOSE voice)'. Compressed bar (INK, label 'CONDITIONING TEXT') appears below with an equals sign. The LLM node (circle, INK) sits below with a downward arrow labeled 'steers the distribution'.

beats.narration_text: These don't add agents. Teardown is not its own agent — it's a parameter of a doer. One writer wearing different costumes. Not many writers. That distinction matters because it changes how you count the moving parts.

production_viz.label: one writer, many costumes — not many writers
production_viz.mechanic: Center: one doer node (INK circle, label 'doer — LLM + goal + tool-loop'). Three thin SLATE rings orbit it, each labeled: 'persona: script-writer', 'register: Teardown', 'brand: NikBearBrown'. They are overlays, not separate boxes. Off to the right (with an 'X' in CRIMSON through it): an alternative diagram showing THREE separate agent nodes labeled 'wrong model — three writers'. Bottom text centered: 'one writer · many costumes' in EB Garamond italic. Then a counter: 'NOT: many writers.'

beats.narration_text: An agent is a composite: LLM plus conditioning text plus a tool loop plus a goal. Two sub-roles. The orchestrator sequences work, owns the contract, hits the gates. The doer does one focused job — write, scout, build, conform. The register the doer wears is not the doer.

props.caption: agent = composite, not monolith
props.code:
class Agent:
    llm          = distribution_over_text
    conditioning = persona + register + brand  # the costume
    tool_loop    = [read, write, run, call]
    goal         = what_to_produce

class Orchestrator(Agent):  # sequences + gates
    owns_contract = True
    hits_gates    = True

class Doer(Agent):          # one focused job
    job = write | scout | build | conform
    # register is a PARAMETER of the doer, not a separate agent

beats.narration_text: Parameters and scripts sit at the bottom. Parameters are exact values — stability 0.80, fps 24, a voice ID. Scripts are deterministic functions: same inputs, same outputs. The shared utility belt — generate_audio.py, remotion_scenes.py, compile.py. Nobody gate-checks them for taste. They don't need it.

production_viz.label: parameters → scripts → deterministic output — no taste gate
production_viz.mechanic: Three script boxes in a horizontal row: 'generate_audio.py', 'remotion_scenes.py', 'compile.py'. Each has an input arrow from the left labeled with parameter examples in PT Mono: 'voice_id, stability' → generate_audio; 'fps=24, width=1920' → remotion_scenes; 'clips/, beat_sheet' → compile. Each has an output arrow to the right: 'deterministic output'. A header bar above: 'THE UTILITY BELT' in Montserrat tracked caps. A strikethrough speech bubble labeled 'taste gate?' in CRIMSON floats above — crossed out — to mark its absence. Everything in INK and SLATE.

beats.narration_text: Three artifact classes, differentiated by who reads and what judgment they invite. The contract — beat_sheet.json — read by agents, approved by you at a gate. Logs — align.json, QC sheets — consulted only when something breaks. The deliverable — the mp4 — judged for taste. The log will never tell you if the video is worth watching.

production_viz.label: contract · logs · deliverable — who reads, what judgment
production_viz.mechanic: Three columns reveal left to right. Column 1 — CONTRACT (CRIMSON header): 'beat_sheet.json' in PT Mono; 'read by: agents + you'; 'judgment: schema check → gate approval'. Column 2 — LOGS (SLATE header): 'align.json · QC sheets · request cards'; 'read by: agents when broken'; 'judgment: none — diagnostic only'. Column 3 — DELIVERABLE (INK header): 'the mp4'; 'read by: you + audience'; 'judgment: TASTE'. Footer under column 3 in small EB Garamond italic: 'The log will never tell you if it's worth watching.'

beats.narration_text: The judgment rule: agents check determinable things — did the script exit clean, does the schema validate, is every beat filled. The human checks judgeable things — is the opening interesting, does the joke land, is it worth watching. Trust an agent to check taste and you've rebuilt AI slop. Taste is structurally underdetermined.

props.command: $ who checks what in the agentic system?
output: ✓ every beat filled      ✓ render matches deck
warning: trust an agent to check taste → AI slop (taste ≠ schema)

beats.narration_text: Back to the puzzle. One instruction, forty files moved. Now you see the split: fps=24 and the voice ID ran deterministically — no gate needed. The persona and the script ran through underdetermined passes, gated for taste. The gates sit at that boundary. Not because the machine is untrustworthy. Because taste is unjudgeable by schema.

production_viz.label: the cascade revisited — SPECIFIES vs INFLUENCES annotated with gates
production_viz.mechanic: The B01 cascade redrawn: beat_sheet → orchestrator → doer → [ElevenLabs | Manim] → compile → mp4. Now two annotation bands overlay: a teal/ink band labeled 'SPECIFIES — no gate' covers fps=24 and voice_id steps; a CRIMSON band labeled 'INFLUENCES — taste gate ▼' covers persona/register steps, each with a small gate icon (a downward triangle) and a human silhouette figure alongside. The mp4 at the end gets a CRIMSON label: 'DELIVERABLE — judged for taste'. The two annotation bands reveal with a wipe, then the gate icons pulse in.

beats.viewer_exercise: Take a system you're building or using. Map every component to the two-axis taxonomy. Where do the human judgment gates sit? Are they placed at every underdetermined step — or are some taste decisions currently delegated to an agent?
beats.narration_text: Maximally informed, minimally autonomous. The line between a specifying input and an influencing input is the same line as between 'the machine owns this' and 'your taste must sign off.' The determinism boundary IS the human-judgment boundary. What's beyond scope: multi-agent scheduling, token allocation, gate structure changes. Start with the taxonomy.

Paste this into Claude, ChatGPT, or Gemini — apply the two-axis taxonomy to a system you're building. Go deeper: where does the taxonomy break down when an artifact from one run becomes a specifying input for the next?

llm_exercise.prompt: I want to understand how to apply the SPECIFIES vs INFLUENCES / INPUTS vs ARTIFACTS taxonomy to real agentic systems. The taxonomy: a specifying input names its exact output (fps=24, a voice ID, a hex code); an influencing input only biases a distribution (a persona, a prompt, a register). Inputs are authored before a run; artifacts are what the run leaves behind. Using this framework, analyze the architecture of a system you're familiar with — a CI/CD pipeline, a customer support bot, a content creation pipeline, or a system of your choice. For each component: (1) Is it a specifying input, an influencing input, or an artifact? (2) What judgment does it invite — schema validation, or human taste? (3) Where should the human judgment gates sit?
llm_exercise.dig_deeper: Go deeper: Where does this taxonomy break down? What happens when an artifact from one run (like a generated script or beat sheet) becomes a specifying input parameter for the next run? Does the SPECIFIES/INFLUENCES distinction still hold, or do you need a different concept to capture that transition?

props.caption: paste into Claude · ChatGPT · Gemini
props.code:
Apply the two-axis taxonomy to a system you're building.

SPECIFIES vs INFLUENCES — does this input name its exact output,
or only bias a distribution?

INPUTS vs ARTIFACTS — authored before the run,
or produced by it?

Map each component. Identify where the
human judgment gates should sit.

Go deeper: where does the taxonomy break down
when an artifact becomes a parameter for the next run?

beats.narration_text: Find the full toolkit at brutalist.art. You bring the taste. The machine brings the hands.
